content:
# Genesis Chapter 27 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15r–15v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_027_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_027_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 23–24 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** When Isaac was old and his eyes were dim so that he could not see, he called Esau his elder son and said to him, "My son." And he said to him, "Here I am."

**2** He said, "Behold, I am old; I do not know the day of my death.

**3** Now then, take up your weapons — your quiver and your bow — and go out to the field and hunt game for me,

**4** and prepare for me a savory dish of the kind I love and bring it to me that I may eat, so that my soul may bless you before I die."

**5** Now Rebekah was listening when Isaac spoke to Esau his son. So Esau went to the field to hunt game and to bring it.

**6** And Rebekah said to Jacob her son, "Behold, I heard your father speaking to Esau your brother,

**7** saying, 'Bring me game and prepare for me a savory dish, that I may eat and bless you before the LORD before my death.'

**8** Now therefore, my son, obey my voice as I command you.

**9** Go to the flock and bring me from there two good young goats, so that I may prepare from them a savory dish for your father, such as he loves.

**10** And you shall bring it to your father to eat, so that he may bless you before his death."

**11** But Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, "Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man.

**12** Perhaps my father will feel me, and I shall seem to him as a deceiver, and I shall bring upon myself a curse and not a blessing."

**13** His mother said to him, "Let your curse be on me, my son. Only obey my voice and go, bring them to me."

**14** So he went and took them and brought them to his mother, and his mother prepared a savory dish of the kind his father loved.

**15** Then Rebekah took the finest garments of Esau her elder son, which were with her in the house, and put them on Jacob her younger son.

**16** And the skins of the young goats she put on his hands and on the smooth part of his neck.

**17** And she gave the savory dish and the bread that she had prepared into the hand of Jacob her son.

**18** So he went in to his father and said, "My father." And he said, "Here I am. Who are you, my son?"

**19** Jacob said to his father, "I am Esau your firstborn. I have done as you told me. Now sit up and eat of my game, so that your soul may bless me."

**20** But Isaac said to his son, "How is it that you have found it so quickly, my son?" He said, "Because the LORD your God granted me success."

**21** Then Isaac said to Jacob, "Come near, that I may feel you, my son, whether you are really my son Esau or not."

**22** So Jacob came near to Isaac his father, who felt him and said, "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau."

**23** And he did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like his brother Esau's hands. And he blessed him.

**24** He said, "Are you really my son Esau?" He said, "I am."

**25** And he said, "Bring it near to me that I may eat of my son's game and bless you." So he brought it near to him, and he ate. And he brought him wine, and he drank.

**26** Then his father Isaac said to him, "Come near and kiss me, my son."

**27** So he came near and kissed him. And he smelled the scent of his garments and blessed him and said:

"See, the smell of my son
is as the smell of a field
that the LORD has blessed.

**28** May God give you of the dew of heaven
and of the fatness of the earth,
and an abundance of grain and wine.

**29** Let peoples serve you,
and nations bow down to you.
Be lord over your brothers,
and may your mother's sons bow down to you.
Cursed be everyone who curses you,
and blessed be everyone who blesses you."

**30** As soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob, when Jacob had scarcely gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, Esau his brother came in from his hunting.

**31** He also prepared a savory dish and brought it to his father. And he said to his father, "Let my father arise and eat of his son's game, that your soul may bless me."

**32** His father Isaac said to him, "Who are you?" He answered, "I am your son, your firstborn, Esau."

**33** Then Isaac trembled with a very great trembling and said, "Who then was it who hunted game and brought it to me, and I ate of it all before you came, and I blessed him? Yes, and he shall be blessed."

**34** When Esau heard the words of his father, he cried out with an exceeding great and bitter cry and said to his father, "Bless me, even me also, O my father!"

**35** But he said, "Your brother came with deceit and took your blessing."

**36** Esau said, "Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times. He took my birthright, and behold, now he has taken my blessing." Then he said, "Have you not reserved a blessing for me?"

**37** Isaac answered and said to Esau, "Behold, I have made him lord over you, and all his brothers I have given to him as servants, and with grain and wine I have sustained him. What then shall I do for you, my son?"

**38** Esau said to his father, "Do you have only one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also, O my father." And Esau lifted up his voice and wept.

**39** Then Isaac his father answered and said to him:

"Behold, away from the fatness of the earth
shall your dwelling be,
and away from the dew of heaven on high.

**40** By your sword you shall live,
and your brother you shall serve.
But when you grow restless,
you shall break his yoke from your neck."

**41** Now Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing with which his father had blessed him, and Esau said in his heart, "The days of mourning for my father are near; then I will kill my brother Jacob."

**42** But the words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah. So she sent and called Jacob her younger son and said to him, "Behold, your brother Esau comforts himself about you by planning to kill you.

**43** Now therefore, my son, obey my voice. Arise, flee to Laban my brother in Haran,

**44** and stay with him a while, until your brother's fury turns away —

**45** until your brother's anger turns away from you and he forgets what you have done to him. Then I will send and bring you from there. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?"

**46** And Rebekah said to Isaac, "I loathe my life because of the Hittite women. If Jacob marries one of the Hittite women such as these, one of the women of the land, what good will my life be to me?"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 27 spans Folios 15r–15v across two pages.

   **Folio 15r** (GEN_027_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 15, presented in normal orientation with the folio number "15" visible in the upper margin. The page carries the end of Genesis 26 (the chapter marker and Esau's Hittite marriages closing the prior chapter) and transitions into Genesis 27. The three-column Tiberian text with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation marks is legible throughout. This folio carries Genesis 27:1 through approximately 27:29 — the full arc of the deception from Isaac's request to the completion of the stolen blessing. The Masoretic parashah markers are visible at appropriate intervals.

   **Folio 15v** (GEN_027_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 15, presented inverted per WSRP imaging convention. This page carries Genesis 27:30 through 27:46 — Esau's return, Isaac's trembling, the discovery, the anti-blessing, Esau's plot, Rebekah's warning and plan, and the closing complaint about the Hittite women. The end of Genesis 27 and beginning of Genesis 28 are visible on this page.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 23** (GEN_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 23 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0023). The chapter marker "CAP. XXX" is visible in the left margin, reflecting the LXX's chapter division system. This page carries the close of Genesis 26 (the Abimelech covenant, Shibah naming, Esau's marriages) and the opening of Genesis 27. The Genesis 27 material on this page covers the opening scene through approximately verse 29, encompassing Isaac's request to Esau, Rebekah's overheard plot, Jacob's deception, and the blessing itself. The continuous three-column uncial text of Vaticanus is legible throughout.

   **Page 24** (GEN_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 24 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0024). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXI" is visible at the top center. This page carries Genesis 27:30 through the end of the chapter and into the beginning of Genesis 28: Esau's return, the discovered deception, Isaac's trembling, the anti-blessing of Esau, Esau's murderous resolve, Rebekah's plan to send Jacob to Haran, and the closing Hittite-women complaint.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "His eyes were dim so that he could not see" (v. 1)**

The Hebrew כָּהוּ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת (kahu einav mire'ot — "his eyes were dim / faded from seeing") uses the verb כָּהָה (kahah, "to be dim, to grow faint, to fail") with the preposition מִן (min, "from") governing the infinitive construct רְאֹת (re'ot, "seeing"). The phrase establishes Isaac's physical blindness as the narrative precondition for everything that follows — the entire chapter hinges on the fact that Isaac cannot see. The formal translation renders "his eyes were dim so that he could not see," following the natural English idiom while preserving the causal-consecutive force. The word מֵרְאֹת could also be rendered "from seeing" (literally), but "so that he could not see" communicates the result clearly. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἠμβλύνθησαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ τοῦ ὁρᾶν (ēmblynthēsan hoi ophthalmoi autou tou horan — "his eyes were blunted/dulled from seeing"), using ἀμβλύνω (amblynō, "to blunt, to dull the edge of"), which is the standard LXX term for failing eyesight.

**2. "My quiver and your bow" / "your weapons" (v. 3)**

The Hebrew כֵּלֶיךָ תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ (kelekha telyekha veqashtekha — literally "your weapons, your quiver and your bow") presents a slight oddity: כֵּלִים (kelim) is the general word for "implements, tools, weapons, utensils," while תֶּלִי (teli) means specifically a quiver (cognate with תָּלָה, "to hang"). The construction lists the general category (weapons) then specifies two components: quiver and bow. The formal translation renders "your weapons — your quiver and your bow," using the dash to convey the appositional clarification in Hebrew. The LXX renders τὴν φαρέτραν σου καὶ τὸ τόξον (tēn pharetra sou kai to toxon — "your quiver and your bow"), omitting the general כֵּלִים and keeping only the specifics.

**3. "That my soul may bless you" (v. 4)**

The phrase וּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי (uvarkekha nafshi — "that my soul may bless you") is one of the chapter's most theologically charged locutions. The word נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh) means "soul, life-force, breath, self, person." The blessing is here attributed not merely to Isaac's words but to his נֶפֶשׁ — the seat of his vital force and being. The idiom implies that the blessing has quasi-physical, nearly magical power: it must come from the living center of the patriarch. When Isaac's vital self (nefesh) confers the blessing, it is bound. This is why the blessing, once given in error to Jacob, cannot be rescinded (v. 33, 37) — it was uttered by a nefesh, and the nefesh-blessing is irrevocable. The formal translation renders "that my soul may bless you" throughout (vv. 4, 19, 25, 31), preserving the repeated נַפְשִׁי formula as a refrain. The LXX renders ἵνα εὐλογήσῃ σε ἡ ψυχή μου (hina eulogēsē se hē psychē mou — "that my soul may bless you"), using ψυχή (psychē) — the standard LXX equivalent for נֶפֶשׁ. The formal translation and LXX agree exactly in this rendering.

**4. "Before the LORD" (v. 7)**

Verse 7 adds a detail not present in Isaac's original speech (v. 4): the blessing is to happen לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (lifnei Adonai — "before the LORD"). This phrase, spoken by Rebekah when she relays Isaac's words to Jacob, may be her interpretation or embellishment — or it may have been understood in Isaac's original speech. It elevates the blessing from a family ceremony to a covenantal act performed in the LORD's presence. The formal translation renders "before the LORD," following the MT. The LXX renders ἐναντίον κυρίου (enantion kyriou — "in the presence of the Lord"), matching the MT sense precisely.

**5. "As a deceiver" / מְתַעְתֵּעַ (v. 12)**

Jacob's fear is that his father will feel him and he will be like מְתַעְתֵּעַ (meta'te'a — "a deceiver, a mocker, one who deludes") in his father's eyes. The root is תָּעָה (ta'ah, "to wander, to err, to lead astray"). The Piel/intensive form in which it appears here (מְתַעְתֵּעַ, a Pilpel or intensive reduplication) conveys the sense of a thorough, deliberate deceiver — not merely someone who is mistaken but someone who actively leads another astray. The formal translation renders "a deceiver," which is the most natural English equivalent for the theological register of the passage. Some translate "a mocker" (KJV) or "one who mocks" (following the LXX's ὡς καταφρονῶν — "as one who despises/mocks"). The LXX rendering ὡς καταφρονῶν (hōs kataphronōn, "as one who despises, treats with contempt") takes a slightly different angle — not deceit but contempt. The formal translation follows the MT's deception sense, which better fits the narrative action (the sin is fraud, not mockery).

**6. "Let your curse be on me" (v. 13)**

Rebekah's response to Jacob's fear of a curse is עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי (alai qillatekha beni — "upon me your curse, my son"). She does not say "there will be no curse" or "God will not curse you" — she absorbs the potential curse into herself. This is an act of extraordinary maternal devotion and also of narrative misdirection: we know from the preceding chapters and from the covenant structure that the blessing will hold and the curse will not come. But Rebekah's willingness to take the curse is striking. The formal translation renders "Let your curse be on me, my son" — preserving the imperative force of עָלַי (alai, "upon me") as a wish/command and the direct address "my son." The LXX renders ἐπ' ἐμέ ἡ κατάρα σου τέκνον (ep' eme hē katara sou teknon — "upon me your curse, child"), matching the MT closely.

**7. "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau" (v. 22)**

This is the most famous line of the chapter and one of the most famous lines in Genesis. The Hebrew is הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו (haqqol qol Ya'aqov vehayyadayim yedei Esav — literally "the voice, the voice of Jacob, and the hands, the hands of Esau"). The double nominalization — "the voice, the voice of..." / "the hands, the hands of..." — creates the iconic parallelism. Each element is doubled for emphasis: "the voice" (haqqol) is first stated as the topic, then identified as specifically Jacob's; "the hands" (hayyadayim) are first stated as the topic, then identified as specifically Esau's. The formal translation renders the parallelism exactly: "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau" — adding only the copula "is" which English requires but Hebrew omits, and "but" to make the contrast explicit. The LXX renders ἡ φωνὴ φωνὴ Ιακωβ, αἱ δὲ χεῖρες χεῖρες Ησαυ (hē phōnē phōnē Iakōb, hai de cheires cheires Hēsau — "the voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau"), matching the MT's double structure precisely. The formal translation follows both sources in their exact parallelism.

**8. The Blessing of Jacob (vv. 27b–29) — Poetic Structure**

Verses 27b–29 are Isaac's blessing of Jacob, which is poetry and is formatted accordingly. The blessing has four movements:
- The olfactory-onomastic opening (v. 27b): the smell of the field the LORD has blessed
- The material blessing (v. 28): dew, earth, grain, wine
- The political blessing (v. 29a–b): lordship over peoples, nations, brothers
- The covenant formula (v. 29c): curse/bless reciprocity

The line וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ בְּנֵי אִמֶּךָ (veyishtachavu lekha benei immekha — "and your mother's sons bow down to you") is notable: it says "your mother's sons" rather than "your brothers" — an unusual formulation. In the parallel blessing of Esau (v. 40) and in the later Jacob-blessing (Genesis 49:8), the formulation varies. The formal translation preserves "your mother's sons" (not "your brothers") as this is what the text says. The LXX renders καὶ προσκυνησάτωσάν σοι υἱοὶ πατρός σου (kai proskynēsatōsan soi hyioi patros sou — "and let the sons of your father bow down to you"), reading "sons of your father" rather than "sons of your mother." This is a significant divergence: the LXX Vorlage or the LXX translator has read or interpreted אָבִיךָ (avikha, "your father") where the MT has אִמֶּךָ (immekha, "your mother"). The formal translation follows the MT: "your mother's sons."

**9. "Isaac trembled with a very great trembling" (v. 33)**

The Hebrew וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד מְאֹד (vayyecherad Yitzhak charadah gedolah ad me'od — "and Isaac trembled a great trembling exceedingly") deploys the cognate accusative construction in which the verb חָרַד (charad, "to tremble, to be terrified") is followed by its cognate noun חֲרָדָה (charadah, "trembling, terror"), then intensified by גְּדֹלָה עַד מְאֹד ("great, unto the very much"). This triple intensification — cognate accusative + great + exceedingly — is one of the strongest emotional emphasis constructions in biblical Hebrew. The formal translation renders "trembled with a very great trembling," preserving the cognate accusative structure (not "trembled greatly" or "was deeply shaken") and the adverbial intensifiers. The LXX renders ἐξέστη Ισαακ ἔκστασιν μεγάλην σφόδρα (exestē Isaak ekstasin megalēn sphodra — "Isaac was beside himself with a great astonishment exceedingly"), also using a cognate accusative (ἔκστασιν from ἐξίστημι) and the same triple intensification. The LXX chooses ἔκστασις (ekstasis — "astonishment, displacement of mind, ecstasy") rather than mere trembling, which shifts the register from physical terror to mental/spiritual dislocation.

**10. "'Is he not rightly named Jacob?'" (v. 36)**

Esau's bitter wordplay: הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַעֲמַיִם (haki qara shmo Ya'aqov vayyaqveni zeh fa'amayim — "Is it not because his name was called Jacob, and he has supplanted me these two times?"). The name יַעֲקֹב (Ya'aqov, Jacob) is from עָקֵב (aqev, "heel") — at the birth scene of Genesis 25:26, Jacob was named because he grabbed his brother's heel (עָקֵב) at birth. Now Esau uses the cognate verb עָקַב (aqav, "to follow at the heel, to supplant, to circumvent") — וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי (vayyaqveni, "he has supplanted me / he has heel-grabbed me"). The formal translation renders "Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times" — preserving the name-to-verb wordplay implicit in "supplanted" (from the same root as "Jacob"). The LXX renders δικαίως ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Ιακωβ· καὶ γὰρ ἐπτέρνικέν με ἤδη δεύτερον τοῦτο (dikaiōs eklēthē to onoma autou Iakōb — "rightly was his name called Jacob; for he has already tripped me a second time"), using πτερνίζω (ptērnizō, "to trip by the heel, to supplant") — a verb that directly maps the Hebrew aqav. The formal translation follows the MT.

**11. The Anti-Blessing of Esau (vv. 39–40) — Poetic Structure**

The "blessing" Isaac gives Esau in vv. 39–40 is in fact an anti-blessing — the inverse of Jacob's blessing in structure and content. Where Jacob received "the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth" (v. 28), Esau receives "away from the fatness of the earth / and away from the dew of heaven" (v. 39). The preposition מִן (min) in מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ (mishmanei ha'arets yihyeh moshavekha) is ambiguous: it can mean either "of / from" (partitive: "of the fatness of the earth shall your dwelling be") or "away from / far from" (privative: "away from the fatness of the earth shall your dwelling be"). Ancient interpreters divided on this: the partitive reading gives Esau a diminished share of material blessing; the privative reading gives him none. Most modern translations (ESV, NASB, NIV) follow the privative reading based on the contrast with Jacob's blessing and the reference to Esau/Edom living in the harsh terrain of Seir. The LXX renders ἀπὸ τῆς πιότητος τῆς γῆς ἔσται ἡ κατοίκησίς σου (apo tēs piotētos tēs gēs estai hē katoikēsis sou — "away from the fatness of the earth shall your habitation be"), clearly using the privative (ἀπό = "away from"). The formal translation renders "away from the fatness of the earth" and "away from the dew of heaven on high," following the privative reading and the LXX.

**12. "When you grow restless" (v. 40)**

The crux of v. 40: וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד וּפָרַקְתָּ עֻלּוֹ מֵעַל צַוָּארֶךָ (vehayah ka'asher tarid ufaragta ullo me'al tzavvarekha — "and it shall be when you break loose / grow restless, you shall break his yoke from your neck"). The verb תָּרִיד (tarid) is from the root רוּד (rud), which means "to roam, to be restless, to wander." The phrase כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד (ka'asher tarid) is often rendered "when you break free" or "when you grow restless" — the sense is that when Edom asserts its independence and strains against Israelite domination, the yoke will break. The formal translation renders "when you grow restless" (following the sense of רוּד as internal striving before external action) over "when you break loose" (which presupposes the action). The LXX renders ὅταν δὲ καθέλῃς (hotan de kathelēs — "but when you break/bring down"), which seems to conflate the condition and the result. The formal translation follows the MT more closely.

**13. "The LORD" Throughout**

The Tetragrammaton יְהוָה (YHWH) appears in Genesis 27 at v. 7 and v. 20 (and implicitly in the blessing formula). It is rendered "the LORD" throughout, consistent with the formal convention established across this translation.

**14. English Name Forms**

Following the project standard, names are rendered in their established English forms: Isaac (יִצְחָק), Rebekah (רִבְקָה), Jacob (יַעֲקֹב), Esau (עֵשָׂו), Haran (חָרָן), Laban (לָבָן). The LXX forms (Ισαακ, Ρεββεκα, Ιακωβ, Ησαυ, Χαρραν, Λαβαν) are consistent with the MT names and present no divergence requiring note.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 27

Pages 23–24 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The LXX's continuous uncial text was legible across both pages. The chapter division marker "CAP. XXXI" is visible at the top of page 24. Key variants observed:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 27:1 | כָּהוּ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת ("his eyes were dim from seeing") | ἠμβλύνθησαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ τοῦ ὁρᾶν ("his eyes were blunted from seeing") | "his eyes were dim so that he could not see" | MT and LXX agree in sense; "dim" follows MT's kahah |
| 27:3 | כֵּלֶיךָ תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ ("your weapons, your quiver and your bow") | τὴν φαρέτραν σου καὶ τὸ τόξον ("your quiver and your bow") | "your weapons — your quiver and your bow" | LXX omits general כֵּלִים; MT preserved |
| 27:7 | לִפְנֵי יְהוָה ("before the LORD") | ἐναντίον κυρίου ("before the Lord") | "before the LORD" | MT and LXX agree |
| 27:12 | מְתַעְתֵּעַ ("a deceiver") | ὡς καταφρονῶν ("as one who despises/mocks") | "a deceiver" | LXX shifts to contempt; MT's deception sense preferred |
| 27:20 | כִּי הִקְרָה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לְפָנַי ("because the LORD your God caused it to happen before me") | ὅτι παρέδωκεν κύριος ὁ θεός σου ἐναντίον μου ("because the Lord your God delivered it before me") | "because the LORD your God granted me success" | LXX uses παραδίδωμι ("to deliver/hand over"); MT uses קָרָה ("to happen, to meet, to grant access") |
| 27:22 | בְּנֵי אִמֶּךָ ("sons of your mother") | υἱοὶ πατρός σου ("sons of your father") | "your mother's sons" | LXX reads/interprets "father" where MT has "mother" — significant divergence; formal translation follows MT |
| 27:33 | חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד מְאֹד ("a great trembling exceedingly") | ἔκστασιν μεγάλην σφόδρα ("a great astonishment exceedingly") | "a very great trembling" | LXX uses ekstasis (mental displacement) rather than physical trembling; formal translation follows MT's physical charadah |
| 27:36 | וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי ("he has supplanted me") | ἐπτέρνικέν με ("he has tripped me at the heel") | "he has supplanted me" | Both preserve the Jacob/heel wordplay; formal translation renders English idiom "supplanted" |
| 27:39–40 | מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ ("of/away from the fatness of the earth") | ἀπὸ τῆς πιότητος ("away from the fatness") | "away from the fatness of the earth" | LXX clearly privative (ἀπό); formal translation follows LXX's privative reading against possible partitive MT reading |
| 27:40 | כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד ("when you grow restless") | ὅταν δὲ καθέλῃς ("when you bring down") | "when you grow restless" | LXX conflates condition and action; formal translation follows MT's רוּד sense |
| 27:46 | קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי ("I loathe my life") | προσώχθισα τῇ ζωῇ μου ("I am disgusted with my life") | "I loathe my life" | MT and LXX agree in sense; LXX's προσοχθίζω ("to be annoyed/disgusted") is strong |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Stolen Blessing — Structure**

Genesis 27 is organized around four major scenes and four major speeches, as the manifest notes:

1. **Isaac to Esau (vv. 1–4):** The charge to hunt game, and the soul-blessing promised.
2. **Rebekah's counterplot (vv. 5–17):** Rebekah overhears, devises the substitute, and prepares Jacob.
3. **Jacob's deception and Isaac's blessing (vv. 18–29):** The encounter, the testing, the blessing conferred.
4. **Esau's return and the aftermath (vv. 30–46):** Discovery, trembling, failed reversal, anti-blessing, Esau's plot, Rebekah's exit plan.

The chapter is held together by the repetition of the נֶפֶשׁ-blessing formula ("that my soul may bless you," vv. 4, 19, 25, 31) and by the irrevocability of the spoken blessing once issued.

**The Irrevocability of the Blessing (v. 33)**

Isaac's response when Esau returns — "Yes, and he shall be blessed" (גַּם בָּרוּךְ יִהְיֶה, gam barukh yihyeh) — is one of the most theologically significant statements in the chapter. The blessing, once spoken by the nefesh of the patriarch, cannot be recalled. This is not because the patriarch is infallible or because the deception is approved; it is because the blessing has passed from the patriarch's nefesh into the world as a spoken act with binding covenantal force. Isaac's trembling is not merely shock at being deceived — it is the trembling of a man who realizes that the covenant-bearing word has already gone from him and attached itself, irreversibly, to the son he did not intend to bless. The formal translation renders the affirmative particle גַּם (gam, "yes, also, indeed") before "and he shall be blessed" — preserving the affirmative force that English "yes" supplies.

**Esau's "Blessing" as Anti-Blessing (vv. 39–40)**

The oracle Isaac gives Esau mirrors Jacob's blessing in structure but inverts its content. Jacob's blessing moves from dew/earth to lordship over nations and brothers. Esau's oracle moves from deprivation of dew/earth to servitude under his brother — with a conditional escape clause (v. 40b). Historically, vv. 39–40 read as a vaticinium ex eventu (prophecy after the fact) describing the relationship between Israel and Edom: Edom was subjugated by David (2 Samuel 8:14) but achieved independence from Jehoram of Israel (2 Kings 8:20–22) — the "breaking of the yoke." The formal translation preserves the poetic structure of both blessings and the symmetrical inversion between them without interpretive intrusion.

**Jacob/Esau and the Covenant**

The narrative presents a theologically uncomfortable scene: the covenant blessing passes to Jacob through fraud. The text does not editorialize or apologize — it simply presents what happened. The formal translation does not soften Jacob's deception ("I am Esau your firstborn," v. 19) or dignify it. Rebekah is the architect of the deception; Jacob is its instrument; Isaac is its victim; and Esau — the one who despised his birthright (Genesis 25:34) — is the figure whose loss the narrative records with sympathy but without surprise. The chapter's moral complexity is part of its literary integrity, and the formal translation preserves it.
